P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Don Excell 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Alameaa County Supervisor 
1221 Oak Street, Suite 536 
Oakland, CA 
94612 
Dea r Mr. Excell: 
Administration 
322-5660 
Executive/legal 
322-5901 
April 18, 1984 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
In your letter of April 9, 1984, you asked if a person 
making an in-kind contribution for the printing of a brochure need 
be identified as the sender of a mass mailing. 
When 200 or more identical or nearly identical pieces of 
campaign literature are mailed within a calendar month, the name of 
the sender must be provided on each envelope and on at least one of 
the inserts. 
"Sender" is defined as the candidate or committee 
which incurs or pays for the largest portion of expenditures 
attributable to the'designing, printing, and posting of the 
mailing. 
In your letter you stated that your committee incurred the 
largest portion of the expenditures for the brochure. 
Therefore, 
the name and address of your committee must be listed on the 
brochure. 
If the brochure is a self-mailer, the name and address 
must appear on the on the outside of the brochure. 
If an envelope 
is used to mail the brochure, the committee's name and address must 
also appear on the envelope. 
The person making the in-kind 
contribution is not required to be identified. 
Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any 
additional questions. 
Sincerely, 
Mary Ann Kvasager 
Political Reform Consultant 
MAK:kt 


BO$.RFb 
DON EXCELL 
SUPERVISOR, FIRST DISTRICT 
April 9, 1984 
Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
ATTN: 
Mary Ann 
SUPERVISORS 
I would appreciate a confirming letter regarding our conversation this date 
about my brochure which was printed as an Ilin-kind'l contribution and had the 
IIpaid for by'l part deleted. 
~~y campa i gn name and number is on the fac i ng page of the brochure and the most 
expensive part of the project will be the labeling, folding and mailing which 
will be paid by my Committee to Re-elect. 
Please confirm the ruling that the in-kind contribution does not have to be 
specifically stated on the face of the brochure, provided its monetary value 
is reported in the regular campaign statement. 
DE/gm 
1221 OAK STREET· SUITE S36 . OAKLAND,CALIFORNIA 94612· (415) 874'7367

sections.question: In your letter of April 9, 1984, you asked if a person making an in-kind contribution for the printing of a brochure need be identified as the sender of a mass mailing.
sections.conclusion: The person making the in-kind contribution is not required to be identified. When 200 or more identical or nearly identical pieces of campaign literature are mailed within a calendar month, the name of the sender (defined as the candidate or committee incurring or paying for the largest portion of expenditures for designing, printing, and posting) must be provided on each envelope and on at least one insert. The committee's name and address must appear on the brochure (on the outside if a self-mailer, or on the envelope if one is used). The person making the in-kind contribution is not required to be identified.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a person making an in-kind contribution for printing a brochure be identified as the sender of a mass mailing when 200+ identical pieces are distributed?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The in-kind contributor does not need to be identified on the brochure. The committee that incurred the largest portion of expenditures must be identified as the sender, and this committee name and address must appear on the mailing materials.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (April 9, 1984 letter from Don Excell) and FPPC response (April 18, 1984 letter from Mary Ann Kvasager). Extraction focuses on the FPPC response as the primary document. Clear question and conclusion sections present.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: In your letter of April 9, 1984, you asked if a person making an in-kind contribution for the printing of a brochure need be identified as the sender of a mass mailing.

CONCLUSION: The person making the in-kind contribution is not required to be identified. When 200 or more identical or nearly identical pieces of campaign literature are mailed within a calendar month, the name of the sender (defined as the candidate or committee incurring or paying for the largest portion of expenditures for designing, printing, and posting) must be provided on each envelope and on at least one insert. The committee's name and address must appear on the brochure (on the outside if a self-mailer, or on the envelope if one is used). The person making the in-kind contribution is not required to be identified.

When 200 or more identical or nearly identical pieces of campaign literature are mailed within a calendar month, the name of the sender must be provided on each envelope and on at least one of the inserts. "Sender" is defined as the candidate or committee which incurs or pays for the largest portion of expenditures attributable to the'designing, printing, and posting of the mailing. If the brochure is a self-mailer, the name and address must appear on the on the outside of the brochure. embedding.summary: FPPC confirms that an in-kind contributor for brochure printing need not be identified as the sender, provided the committee incurring the largest portion of costs is properly identified on the mailing with required sender identification.
